message:
feat(fp-types): Timestamp struct + Timedelta arithmetic (br-9p0u / 4r56 Phase 2)

Closes br-frankenpandas-9p0u (4r56 Phase 2). Adds nanosecond-precision Timestamp struct to fp-types paired with the Timedelta arithmetic shipped in 4r56 Phase 1.

- pub struct Timestamp { nanos: i64, tz: Option<String> }. Serde round-trip with tz skipped when None. NaT sentinel is i64::MIN (parallel to Timedelta::NAT).
- Constructors: Timestamp::from_nanos(nanos) and Timestamp::from_nanos_tz(nanos, tz_name). tz is opaque metadata in Phase 2 — Phase 3 will interpret it via chrono_tz.
- NaT sentinel: Timestamp::nat() + is_nat() helper.
- Arithmetic: add_timedelta, sub_timedelta, sub_timestamp (returns Timedelta nanos). Saturation on overflow; NaT propagation. TZ is preserved on add_timedelta / sub_timedelta results.
- semantic_eq: NaT == NaT returns true (follows fp-types convention, not pandas == semantics which return False).
- PartialOrd: orders by nanos; NaT returns None (incomparable).
- Display: Phase 2 debug format Timestamp[nanos, tz-or-UTC]. Phase 3 replaces with pandas ISO-8601 once chrono lands in fp-types.

13 unit tests cover: construction (naive + tz-tagged), arithmetic (add/sub td, sub timestamp), NaT propagation on both sides, overflow saturation, Display format, partial_cmp with NaT incomparability, serde round-trip, compile-time Send+Sync assertion.

rch: cargo test -p fp-types --lib timestamp_ (13 pass), cargo check --workspace --all-targets clean, cargo clippy -p fp-types --all-targets -D warnings clean, ubs exit 0.

Phase 3 (chrono_tz integration for DST-aware arithmetic + ISO-8601 Display + strftime) deferred. fp-types stays chrono-free in Phase 2.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -9,7 +9,7 @@
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0iyb","title":"[HIGH] ci: no concurrency: cancel-in-progress group — rapid PR pushes trigger duplicate CI runs (5-8x waste per PR)","description":"CI concurrency audit.

**Observation.** `.github/workflows/*.yml` — zero matches for `concurrency:` or `cancel-in-progress:`.

**What this means.** When an author pushes commit A to a PR branch, then pushes commit B 30 seconds later (fixup, rebase, etc.), GitHub Actions triggers TWO runs: one for A, one for B. Both fully run through all jobs (checks, fuzz-regression, etc.) even though only run B's result is relevant.

**Swarm-specific amplification.** Today's git log shows 20+ commits across 6 hours from multiple agents (cc-pandas + Dicklesworthstone + parallel workers + auto-rebase workflows). Every session with multiple rapid commits doubles, triples, or quadruples the CI load for no additional signal. br-0848138's `br flush` commit + my `pass-11` commit would trigger 2 runs; the `br flush` result doesn't matter because pass-11 is newer.

Rough math:
- Single PR with typical 5-8 commits: without concurrency group → 5-8 full CI runs (5-8 × ~7 min = 35-56 runner minutes).
- With concurrency group → only the final push's run completes (~7 min).
- Ratio: 5-8x waste.
- Swarm has ~20 commits/day × 7 min/run = 140 min/day CI cycles spent on superseded work.

**The standard fix.** Three lines at the top of each workflow:
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true
```

`group` identifies "same workflow, same branch/PR"; `cancel-in-progress: true` cancels the still-running older run when a new push lands. Exception on the main branch: use `cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}` so pushes to main never cancel each other (main branch runs are archival).

**Severity.** HIGH — compound cost across every PR in perpetuity. At this swarm's velocity, easily the cheapest bead in the backlog to pay off.

**Fix shape:**

1. Add `concurrency:` block to `.github/workflows/ci.yml` at workflow level:
   ```yaml
   name: CI
   on: [push, pull_request]

   concurrency:
     group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
     cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

   jobs:
     checks:
       ...
   ```

2. If any future workflow is added (br-4clx release workflow, br-36qc security workflow, br-ing6 nightly perf), each needs its own `concurrency:` group — or share the `group:` so they serialize / cancel together.

3. Consider a tighter group key for PR runs:
   ```yaml
   concurrency:
     group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
     cancel-in-progress: true
   ```
   The `pull_request.number || github.ref` idiom groups by PR number on PR events (always unique per PR) and by ref on direct pushes. Belt-and-suspenders version.

Blast radius: ci.yml + any future workflow file, 3-4 lines each. Zero source changes, zero test changes.

Prerequisites: None. Ship in isolation.

**Related:**
- br-ffhs (CI job splitting) — split jobs each need their own `concurrency:` group OR share one. Coordinate the two PRs.
- br-0l5r (cross-platform matrix) — matrix jobs should ALSO inherit the concurrency cancel so one matrix cell doesn't get canceled while another proceeds (generally fine with the above — whole workflow cancels).
- br-4clx (release workflow) — ships a new workflow, needs its own concurrency block.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:24:46.716318082Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T16:44:08.653341Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T16:44:08.653341Z","close_reason":"Already satisfied on main: ci.yml workflow-level concurrency block with PR-aware cancel-in-progress is in place since ffhs CI split","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","concurrency","review","waste"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0jmn","title":"pandas: Series.reset_index(name=...) value-column naming parity","description":"Python signature: pd.Series.reset_index(level=None, drop=False, name=..., inplace=False, allow_duplicates=<no_default>). Current fp-frame Series.reset_index only accepts drop: bool and cannot override the value column name on the DataFrame-return path. Implement the name override for single-index Series, preserve the existing drop=True Series branch, and add focused tests for named series, unnamed series, and collision handling with index/index-name columns.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:59:59.749325004Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:45:26.544815036Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:45:26.543000433Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0kb3","title":"fp-columnar Column.astype parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T06:59:50.285667679Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T07:10:15.788534132Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T07:10:15.786415942Z","close_reason":"Implemented Column::astype in commit 88836d2, 5 tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"frankenpandas-0kx7","title":"[MEDIUM] parity: GroupBy.apply result-shape semantics vs pandas 2.x","description":"Parity-gap audit (/reality-check-for-project).

**Scope.** pandas `DataFrame.groupby(keys).apply(func)` calls a user-provided function per group, returns a Series or DataFrame with the group keys as index. Pandas 2.x changed default `include_groups=True→False`; the result shape depends on what `func` returns.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-frame has `DataFrame::groupby(...).apply(closure)` — surface check:

```
grep -n "pub fn apply\|GroupBy.*apply" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs  # find fn signatures
```

Gaps to verify:
- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a DataFrame (pandas concatenates, preserving group key index).
- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a Series of different length per group (pandas allows, produces sparse output).
- `.apply(func, include_groups=False)` — pandas 2.x default.
- Returning a scalar: pandas collapses to `Series` indexed by group keys.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — `.apply` is widely used for custom group logic; silent divergence from pandas result shape breaks user pipelines.

**Fix shape.**
1. Audit `DataFrameGroupBy::apply_*` signatures in fp-groupby.
2. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_groupby_apply.rs (10 inputs: empty groups, single-row groups, scalar-return, Series-return, DataFrame-return, mixed dtypes, ordered vs unordered groups, duplicate group labels, NaN group labels, `include_groups=False`).
3. DISCREPANCIES.md entry for any intentional shape divergence.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:25.394565113Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:25.394565113Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["apply","groupby","parity-gap","review"]}

3. DISCREPANCIES.md entry for any intentional shape divergence.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cod-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:11:25.394565113Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T03:22:56.613868144Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["apply","groupby","parity-gap","review"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-0l5r","title":"[HIGH] ci: ubuntu-latest only — macOS/Windows cross-platform claim unverified","description":"Cross-platform CI audit.

**Observation.** .github/workflows/ci.yml line 10: `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`. No `strategy.matrix` defining a per-OS test matrix. No macOS runner. No Windows runner.

**Implications.**
- fp-io reads/writes Excel (.xlsx, .xls), CSV, Parquet, Feather, Arrow IPC. CSV line-ending semantics (LF vs CRLF) differ across OS. Excel / Parquet zip-archive handling via calamine / parquet-crate depends on OS-level file layer on some paths. None of this is tested on Windows, where the failure modes would appear.
- Path separators: fp-io has pub fn read_csv(path: &Path), pub fn read_parquet(path: &Path), etc. On Windows, a fixture path string produced on Linux with "/" may or may not resolve correctly depending on canonicalization. Not tested.
- Endianness: arrow / parquet byte manipulation is all little-endian; future big-endian fixtures or a PowerPC build would regress silently.
- The README advertises "Cross-platform (Linux/macOS/Windows)" under Installation implicitly by describing standard cargo build. Nothing validates that claim.

**Severity.** HIGH — product claim (cross-platform Rust library) is unverified. A Windows user of frankenpandas today is running untested code on every IO surface.

**Fix shape:**

ci.yml gains:
```
jobs:
  checks:
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    ...
```

Caveats and cost:
- Windows runners are ~2x slower than Ubuntu. Initial run will take ~12 min instead of 6.
- rch (remote-build helper) likely won't exist on Windows runners. Windows CI would build locally via `cargo test --workspace`.
- cargo-llvm-cov (installed but unused per sibling bead) works on macOS, limited on Windows; Windows job may need to skip the install.
- Some tests may rely on POSIX behavior: `std::fs::set_permissions` chmod bits, symlinks, etc. Expect 0-5 failures on the first Windows run that need per-OS cfg-gating or fixture adjustments. These become trackable beads.
- Excel path resolution on Windows uses \ separators; calamine's `open_workbook` may handle this but needs verification.

Incremental path if 3-OS matrix is too aggressive at once:
1. Start: ubuntu + macos (rch-compatible POSIX).
2. Next: add windows with only a `cargo check` + `cargo test -p fp-types -p fp-columnar -p fp-index` (no IO) to surface path-handling bugs without full IO breakage.
3. Finally: full `cargo test --workspace --all-targets` on windows.

Related: per-OS arrow/parquet/calamine versions don't diverge today (one workspace.dependencies block) — so any divergence in test behavior is a code bug, not a dep bug.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-22T19:59:30.233193403Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T16:23:19.122223754Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T16:23:19.119724019Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","cross-platform","review"]}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-8opp","title":"[LOW] citation: no CITATION.cff — scientific users can't mechanically cite frankenpandas in papers","description":"Academic-citation metadata audit.

**Observation.** `ls CITATION.cff` → No such file. No README section on "how to cite this in a paper." No DOI / Zenodo integration.

**Why this matters for a pandas-parity library.** pandas itself is cited in ~50k academic papers per year — it's the canonical data-analysis tool in scientific Python. frankenpandas positions itself as "clean-room Rust reimplementation of the full pandas API surface" targeting the same user base. Scientific users WILL want to cite the library in their methods sections once adoption grows.

**What CITATION.cff is.** YAML-format standard for software citation metadata. GitHub auto-detects the file and renders a "Cite this repository" button + generates BibTeX / APA / etc. on demand.

**Severity.** LOW — no functional impact, no release blocker, no swarm-velocity friction. But: if we ship 0.1.0 to crates.io without a citation format, the first academic user to adopt the library opens an issue asking "how do I cite this?" — easy to preempt.

**Fix shape.** `CITATION.cff` at repo root:
```yaml
cff-version: 1.2.0
message: "If you use frankenpandas in published work, please cite it."
title: "frankenpandas: clean-room Rust reimplementation of the pandas API"
authors:
  - family-names: "Doodle"
    given-names: "Jeffrey E"    # or whichever upstream maintainer names
    orcid: ""                    # optional
repository-code: "https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas"
url: "https://frankenpandas.dev"
license: MIT
version: "0.1.0"
date-released: "2026-XX-XX"       # fill in at release
keywords:
  - dataframe
  - pandas
  - rust
  - data-analysis
  - columnar-storage
preferred-citation:
  type: software
  authors: <as above>
  title: "frankenpandas"
  url: "https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas"
```

Once GitHub renders the "Cite this repository" button, academic users get BibTeX they can paste into papers. Zero ongoing maintenance beyond version bumps at release.

Optional companion: Zenodo integration. Push a release tag → Zenodo mints a DOI → CITATION.cff updates with the DOI → papers can cite the specific version. Free; takes ~5 min of setup once.

Blast radius: 1 new file (~20 lines). Zero source changes. Zero CI changes.

Prerequisites: None. Ship in isolation.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release workflow) — CITATION.cff's version field updates per release; consider automation.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — "How do I cite this?" section links to CITATION.cff.
- README capability table — "Cite this repository" GitHub button is a free marketing surface.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T22:01:08.609599866Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T17:51:18.242696Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T17:51:18.242696Z","close_reason":"CITATION.cff already shipped on main.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["academic","citation","hygiene","review"]}

-{"id":"frankenpandas-8qdg","title":"[MEDIUM] parity: Categorical ordered comparison + sort semantics","description":"Parity-gap audit: Categorical dtype + ordered comparisons.

**Scope.** pandas `pd.Categorical(values, categories=['low','med','high'], ordered=True)` supports:
- `cat1 < cat2` element-wise comparison (valid only on ordered Categoricals).
- `cat.sort_values()` respects category order, not lex.
- `cat.min() / .max()` respects category order.
- Unordered Categorical: comparison raises TypeError.
- `cat.reorder_categories(new_order, ordered=True)` permits comparison after reorder.
- `pd.concat([cat1, cat2])`: if categories differ, promotes to object dtype (pandas 2.x) — DISC candidate.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-frame has `CategoricalAccessor` + `CategoricalMetadata` (re-exported via crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs:29). Check:

```
grep -n "ordered\|is_ordered\|CategoricalMetadata" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs | head
grep -n "categorical.*cmp\|cat.*compare" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs | head
```

Gaps to verify:
- Does ordered comparison respect category order or fall back to lex / underlying codes?
- Does `.sort_values` on ordered Categorical respect category order?
- Does `.min() / .max()` on ordered Categorical return the earliest/latest category, not lex min/max of values?
- Unordered Categorical `<` behavior: does it return Err or silently produce bogus result?

**Severity.** MEDIUM — Categorical is claimed in README capability table as "metadata layer"; ordered semantics are the point.

**Fix shape.**
1. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_categorical_ordered.rs: 10 inputs covering ordered cmp, unordered cmp rejection, sort_values on ordered, min/max on ordered, reorder_categories then compare, concat with different categories, NaN in categorical, empty categorical, single-category ordered cmp, ordered vs tz-aware (rejection expected).
2. DISCREPANCIES entries for any intentional pandas-2.x divergence.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-25T02:12:47.053950440Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T02:12:47.053950440Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["categorical","ordered","parity-gap","review"]}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-9my2","title":"pandas/io/excel: read_excel(sheet_name=[...]) selected-sheet dict parity","description":"py signature:
pandas.read_excel(io, sheet_name=["Sheet1", "Sheet2"], header=0, index_col=None, skiprows=0) -> dict[str, DataFrame]

Current fp-io only supports one sheet name. +{"id":"frankenpandas-9p0u","title":"fp-types Timestamp struct + Timedelta arithmetic (4r56 Phase 2)","description":"Phase 2 of 4r56 (Timedelta arithmetic shipped in commit 68e2087).

**Scope.** Add a `Timestamp` struct to fp-types providing:
- Nanosecond-precision i64 storage (Unix epoch anchored).
- NaT sentinel (i64::MIN) for missingness.
- Arithmetic: `Timestamp + Timedelta -> Timestamp`, `Timestamp - Timestamp -> Timedelta`, `Timestamp - Timedelta -> Timestamp`.
- Saturation on overflow; NaT propagation.
- Serde round-trip + semantic_eq.

**Non-scope (deferred to Phase 3).**
- TZ-aware Timestamp: a `tz: Option<String>` field is reserved now, but TZ-dependent arithmetic (DST transitions, tz conversion via chrono_tz) lands in a separate slice that pulls chrono_tz into fp-types's dep graph.
- ISO 8601 parsing / strftime display: chrono integration stays in downstream crates; Phase 2 ships constructor-from-nanos only.
- Display: Phase 2 uses a debug-ish format `"Timestamp[<nanos>, UTC]"`; pandas notation lands in Phase 3.

**Blast radius.** Purely additive in fp-types (no existing call-site touched). Zero dep-graph change (no chrono in fp-types yet).

**Tests (10):**
- Construction from nanos.
- Timestamp + Timedelta arithmetic.
- Timestamp - Timestamp = Timedelta.
- Timestamp - Timedelta arithmetic.
- NaT propagation (Timestamp or Timedelta is NaT).
- Saturation on overflow.
- semantic_eq NaT-aware.
- Ordering (non-NaT comparison respects chronological order).
- Serde round-trip.
- Compile-time Send+Sync assertion.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-25T03:23:53.821472519Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T03:26:20.881490680Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T03:26:20.879041374Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["dtype","parity-gap","review","timestamp"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-9q98","title":"[HIGH] REVIEW: mixed naive/offset to_datetime utc parity drift","description":"Found while building frankenpandas-ow57 datetime conformance matrix.

Failing input: series_to_datetime, datetime_utc=true, values=["2024-01-15 10:30:00", "2024-01-15 10:30:00+05:30"].

Expected from live pandas oracle: ["2024-01-15 10:30:00+00:00", null].

Actual from FrankenPandas: ["2024-01-15 10:30:00+00:00", "2024-01-15 05:00:00+00:00"].

Impact: pandas inferred-format coercion rejects the offset-aware second value in this mixed naive/offset sequence, while FrankenPandas accepts and normalizes it.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T06:06:41.985402560Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T07:41:32.596622149Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T07:41:32.594733021Z","close_reason":"Fixed to_datetime mixed naive/offset UTC parity drift","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["datetime","parity","review"]}
